coredata.dc:title: Surface charge effects of carbon quantum dots on oxidative stress, inflammation, and intestinal barrier integrity: An in vitro and in vivo combination study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yi-Chun
Hong, Yan-Yu
Yu, Hsin Her
Liu, Kuei-Chun
Wang, Da-Li
Kuo, Wen-Kai
Wang, Ya-Wen
Lin, Chia-Hua
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) hold promise for biomedical applications; however, their cytotoxicity and the effects they may have on the intestines remain unclear. In this study we evaluated CQDs with different surface charges using Caco-2 cells and BALB/c mice, assessing the cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, inflammation, and intestinal toxicity. Physicochemical analysis revealed distinct surface properties of diammonium citrate-based (CQDs-A), spermidine trihydrochloride-based (CQDs-S), and mixed (CQDs-A/S) CQDs, which influence the biological interactions. CQDs-S exhibited the highest cytotoxicity, reducing cell viability, inducing mitochondrial dysfunction, and triggering apoptosis. It also caused a 40-fold increase in ROS at 1000 μg/mL, activating oxidative stress and inflammatory pathways (HO-1, NF-κB). CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S disrupted intestinal barrier integrity, with mild reductions in zonula occludens 2 and Alpha-1 antitrypsin protein expression and transient transepithelial electrical resistance fluctuations, suggesting potential risks for inflammatory bowel disease-related dysfunction. In vivo, CQDs-S altered the calcium and phosphorus homeostasis; however, did not cause structural intestinal damage. CQDs-A showed the highest biocompatibility among the three CQDs. These findings demonstrate that the surface charge is a key factor in CQD toxicity, emphasizing the necessity for enhanced design to minimize adverse effects. Further studies are required to determine the long-term intestinal permeability, gut microbiome interactions, and chronic inflammation associated with the use of CQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Oxidative stress
Intestinal barrier integrity
Cytotoxicity
Inflammatory response
Surface charge effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Serum analysis of calcium (CA), phosphorus (P), and magnesium (MG) in mice
Cytotoxic effects on Caco-2 cells after exposure to CQDs
Oxidative and inflammatory responses after exposure to CQDs in Caco-2 cells
Intestinal dysfunction after exposure to CQDs in Caco-2 cells
CQDs induced injury in the intestine of BALB/c mice

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Cytotoxicity and mitochondrial integrity of Caco-2 cells exposed to CQDs with different surface charges. (A) Cell viability of Caco-2 cells after 24 h of exposure to different concentrations (0.1–1000 μg/mL) of CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S, as assessed using the CCK-8 assay. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation of three determinations. *indicates p < 0.05 as compared with control. (B) Fluorescence images of Caco-2 cells stained with JC-1 dye to assess mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm). (a) Control cells. (b) CQDs-A treatment. (c) CQDs-S treatment. (d) CQDs-A/S treatment. (e) A positive control. Scale bars = 50 μm.

Oxidative stress and HO-1 protein expression in Caco-2 cells exposed to CQDs with different surface charges. (A) Intracellular ROS accumulation in Caco-2 cells after 24 h of exposure to CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S at concentrations of 10, 100, and 1000 μg/mL. ROS levels were measured using the DCFH-DA assay and expressed as fold change relative to the control. (B) HO-1 protein expression in Caco-2 cells following 24-hour exposure to CQDs at 10 and 100 μg/mL, analyzed via western blot. Representative blot images display HO-1 and β-actin (loading control) levels for each treatment group. The corresponding quantification (bottom panel) shows HO-1 expression normalized to the control. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation of three determinations. *indicates p < 0.05 as compared with control.

NF-κB protein expression in Caco-2 cells exposed to CQDs with different surface charges. NF-κB protein expression in Caco-2 cells following 24-hour exposure to CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S at 10 and 100 μg/mL, analyzed via western blot. Representative blot images display NF-κB and β-actin (loading control) levels for each treatment group (top panel). The corresponding quantification (bottom panel) shows NF-κB expression normalized to the control. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation of three determinations. *indicates p < 0.05 as compared with control.

Effects of CQDs on intestinal barrier function in Caco-2 cells. (A) ZO-2 protein expression in Caco-2 cells after 24-hour exposure to CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S at 10 and 100 μg/mL, analyzed via western blot. Representative blot images display ZO-2 and β-actin (loading control) levels for each treatment group (top panel). The corresponding quantification (bottom panel) shows ZO-2 expression normalized to the control. (B) Transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) measurements in Caco-2 cells treated with CQDs-A (red), CQDs-S (green), and CQDs-A/S (blue) at 10 μg/mL over a 3-day period.

AAT protein expression in Caco-2 cells exposed to CQDs with different surface charges. AAT protein expression in Caco-2 cells following 24-hour exposure to CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S at 10 and 100 μg/mL, analyzed via western blot. Representative blot images display AAT and β-actin (loading control) levels for each treatment group (top panel). The corresponding quantification (bottom panel) shows AAT expression normalized to the control. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation of three determinations. *indicates p < 0.05 as compared with control.

Effects of CQDs on serum calcium, phosphorus, and magnesium levels in BALB/c mice. BALB/c mice were administered CQDs-A, CQDs-S, or CQDs-A/S at a dose of 2 g/kg, and blood samples were collected 24 h post-administration to assess serum mineral levels. (A) Serum calcium (Ca) levels. (B) Serum phosphorus (P) levels. (C) Serum magnesium (Mg) levels. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation of three determinations. *indicates p < 0.05 as compared with control.

Histopathological assessment of the colon and duodenum in BALB/c mice following CQD exposure. Hematoxylin and Eosin (H&E) staining of colon and duodenum sections from BALB/c mice 24 h after an acute toxicity test with CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S at a dosage of 2 g/kg. (A, E) Control group. (B, F) CQDs-A group. (C, G) CQDs-S group. (D, H) CQDs-A/S group. Scale bars = 200 μm.

head.ce:title: Surface charge effects of carbon quantum dots on oxidative stress, inflammation, and intestinal barrier integrity: An in vitro and in vivo combination study

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biology, University of Michigan-Flint, MI, 48502, USA
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Biology, University of Michigan-Flint, MI, 48502, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, National Formosa University, Yunlin, 63208, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Biotechnology, National Formosa University, Yunlin, 63208, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Graduate Institute of Electro-Optical and Materials Science, National Formosa University, Yunlin, 63208, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate Institute of Electro-Optical and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cGraduate Institute of Electro-Optical and Materials Science, National Formosa University, Yunlin, 63208, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) hold promise for biomedical applications; however, their cytotoxicity and the effects they may have on the intestines remain unclear. In this study we evaluated CQDs with different surface charges using Caco-2 cells and BALB/c mice, assessing the cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, inflammation, and intestinal toxicity. Physicochemical analysis revealed distinct surface properties of diammonium citrate-based (CQDs-A), spermidine trihydrochloride-based (CQDs-S), and mixed (CQDs-A/S) CQDs, which influence the biological interactions. CQDs-S exhibited the highest cytotoxicity, reducing cell viability, inducing mitochondrial dysfunction, and triggering apoptosis. It also caused a 40-fold increase in ROS at 1000 μg/mL, activating oxidative stress and inflammatory pathways (HO-1, NF-κB). CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S disrupted intestinal barrier integrity, with mild reductions in zonula occludens 2 and Alpha-1 antitrypsin protein expression and transient transepithelial electrical resistance fluctuations, suggesting potential risks for inflammatory bowel disease-related dysfunction. In vivo, CQDs-S altered the calcium and phosphorus homeostasis; however, did not cause structural intestinal damage. CQDs-A showed the highest biocompatibility among the three CQDs. These findings demonstrate that the surface charge is a key factor in CQD toxicity, emphasizing the necessity for enhanced design to minimize adverse effects. Further studies are required to determine the long-term intestinal permeability, gut microbiome interactions, and chronic inflammation associated with the use of CQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), recognized for their distinct physical and chemical attributes, which include intense photoluminescence, remarkable aqueous dispersibility, and compatibility with biological systems, are increasingly being considered as a new class of high-potential nanomaterial. Thus, they have been extensively used in bioimaging, drug delivery, biosensing, and antimicrobial applications (Chahal et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020; Poonia et al., 2025; Su et al., 2020; Yan et al., 2023). CQDs have demonstrated potential as fluorescent probes for the real-time imaging of cells and tissues, providing a nontoxic alternative to conventional dyes and quantum dots containing heavy metals (Liu et al., 2020; Naik et al., 2022). In addition, their ability to act as carriers for targeted drug delivery has been examined, particularly for cancer therapy, where functionalized CQDs can enhance drug solubility and specificity (Bhattacharya et al., 2024; Naik et al., 2022). In addition to their biomedical applications, CQDs have been utilized in energy conversion, photocatalysis, and environmental remediation (Chahal et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2020). Their ability to serve as photocatalysts in pollutant degradation and water purification processes has attracted interest in environmental science (Chahal et al., 2021; Nammahachak et al., 2022). Given their extensive applicability, understanding the biological interactions and potential toxicological risks of CQDs, especially in biomedical settings, remains an area of focus in the field.

The gastrointestinal tract serves as a primary route for the absorption of carbon-based nanomaterials (Chen et al., 2018; Kreyling et al., 2009), whether through direct ingestion, leaching from food containers, or secondary exposure via contaminated water, as well as the unintentional consumption of affected animals or fish (Li et al., 2020). Recent research focuses on examining the effects of carbon-based nanomaterials on intestinal inflammation and gut microbiota (Jia et al., 2023). In vitro investigations into the gastrointestinal effects of carbon nanomaterials have exhibited significant cytotoxicity when carbon nanomaterials are introduced into Caco-2 cells (Jos et al., 2009; Liu et al., 2013). However, in colonic cells these nanoparticles exhibit negligible impacts on intestinal membrane integrity (Pelka et al., 2013). DNA damage and mitochondrial activity impairment have indicated cellular stress and injury (Pelka et al., 2013). Numerous studies have highlighted that carbon nanomaterials induce oxidative stress by upregulating ROS, ultimately resulting in apoptosis (Isakovic et al., 2006; Mohammadinejad et al., 2019). Prior research has also shown that certain nanoparticles can disrupt tight junction proteins, such as the zonula occludens, thereby increasing intestinal permeability and contributing to systemic inflammation (Vitulo et al., 2022). The precise effects of CQDs on intestinal cells remain unelucidated.

The production of ROS and the subsequent induction of oxidative stress are widely recognized as critical indicators of nanoparticle-induced toxicity and inflammation (Manke et al., 2013; Xuan et al., 2023). Most ROS are formed as byproducts of mitochondrial electron transport processes (Zhao et al., 2019). Molecular oxygen contains two unpaired electrons in separate orbitals of its outer shell, a configuration that predisposes it to form free radicals (Amir, 2021). An overabundance of ROS may trigger oxidative stress and cause cellular damage and DNA lesions, triggering either cell survival mechanisms or apoptotic pathways, depending on the intensity and duration of the exposure (Villalpando-Rodriguez and Gibson, 2021). In addition, ROS buildup plays a crucial role in modulating intracellular signaling cascades, particularly by triggering p38 mitogen-activated protein kinase and nuclear factor kappa B (NF-κB), which govern processes such as cell growth, cell specialization, and programmed cell death (Saha et al., 2007). Dysregulation of NF-κB signaling enhances the expression of various proinflammatory cytokines, such as TNF-α, IL-1, and IL-8, within intestinal epithelial cells, thereby exacerbating inflammation and contributing to carcinogenic processes (Liu et al., 2022).

Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is a chronic and recurrent gastrointestinal disorder characterized by intestinal bleeding and mucosal erosion (Wang et al., 2023). During the progression of IBD, excessive ROS production triggers oxidative stress and proteolytic enzyme activation caused by infiltrating neutrophils, which target endothelial cells and induce cellular damage (Mittal et al., 2014). This process compromises the intestinal mucosal barrier, allowing luminal pathogens to invade, exacerbating inflammatory cell infiltration and tissue damage (An et al., 2022; Okumura and Takeda, 2025). Ultimately, these events result in mucosal necrosis and ulceration. People diagnosed with IBDs such as ulcerative colitis and Crohn’s disease, have a heightened risk of colorectal cancer (Odze, 2003). As the disease persists, the associated risk escalates, with extended inflammation further exacerbating the pathological changes. Although the precise etiology and pathophysiology of IBD remain incompletely understood, in vitro studies and animal models have significantly contributed to the current understanding of its onset and inflammatory mechanisms. The key factors implicated in IBD pathogenesis include genetic predisposition, immune system dysregulation, oxidative stress, intestinal epithelial barrier dysfunction, and environmental influences (Kalafateli et al., 2025).

Alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT), a major acute-phase plasma protein primarily synthesized in the liver, and serves as a potent inhibitor of neutrophil elastase (Janciauskiene et al., 2018). Prior research has revealed that AAT is present in human intestinal epithelial cells, as AAT mRNA has been detected in the human jejunum via RNA analysis, and its synthesis has been observed in human colorectal adenocarcinoma Caco-2 cells (Molmenti et al., 1993; Perlmutter et al., 1989). Local tissue damage in the intestine is often attributed to an imbalance between specific proteases and their inhibitors, with AAT playing a crucial regulatory role. AAT exhibits potent anti-inflammatory effects by inhibiting immune cell activation and lowering the secretion of proinflammatory cytokines, thereby modulating the inflammatory response (Mazzuca et al., 2024; McElvaney et al., 2020). Studies have shown that AAT alleviates inflammatory symptoms in both small and large intestinal IBD models by mitigating proinflammatory cytokine secretion, limiting inflammatory cell infiltration, and minimizing tissue damage (Al-Omari et al., 2023; Collins et al., 2013). In addition, AAT plays a critical role in reducing intestinal permeability, forming part of the protective mechanism in both acute and chronic IBD models (Collins et al., 2013). Although AAT deficiency is primarily associated with pulmonary and hepatic pathologies, evidence suggests that patients with AAT deficiency who also suffer from IBD tend to develop a more aggressive disease, often requiring surgical intervention at an accelerated rate (Collins et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2000a).

In this study, we performed a series of in vitro and in vivo experiments to evaluate the potential nephrotoxicity associated with different surface charges and elevated concentrations of CQDs. Specifically, we examined CQDs synthesized with diammonium citrate (CQDs-A), spermidine trihydrochloride (CQDs-S), and their combination (CQDs-A/S) to assess their biological impacts. Our findings contribute to the growing body of literature on nanoparticle toxicity by providing insights into how CQD surface properties affect intestinal epithelial cell viability, oxidative stress responses, and inflammatory signaling. This research is particularly relevant for evaluating the safety of CQDs in biomedical applications and developing strategies to mitigate their potential adverse effects on the intestinal system.

Caco-2 human colorectal adenocarcinoma cells were maintained at 37 °C with 5 % CO₂ in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) containing 10 % FBS in a humidified incubator. The culture medium was replaced three times per week, and the cells were passaged via trypsinization every 7 days.

CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S were suspended in DMEM and evaluated for cytotoxicity using a Cell Counting Kit-8 (Dojindo, MD, USA). Caco-2 cells were seeded at a density of 8 × 10³ cells per well in a 96-well plates and treated with different concentrations of CQDs (0.1–1000 µg/mL) for 24 h. After incubation, 10 µL of the CCK-8 solution was added to each well, followed by further incubation at 37 °C. The optical density at 450 nm was then recorded using a microplate reader.

Caco-2 cells were seeded at a density of 8 × 10³ cells per well in black 96-well plates and incubated overnight. The following day, the cells were treated with carboxy 2ʹ,7ʹ- dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate-labeled CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S at concentrations of 10, 100, and 1000 µg/mL for 24 h. ROS generation was quantified using a Twinkle LB fluorescence spectrometer (Berthold Industries Ltd, UK) with the excitation and emission wavelengths set at 485 and 535 nm, respectively. Hydrogen peroxide served as the positive control.

Western blotting was conducted to assess the expression levels of proteins related to oxidative stress, inflammation, and liver pathology. Caco-2 cells were seeded at a density of 1.42 × 10⁴ cells per 10-cm culture dish and exposed to CQDs-A, CQDs-S, or CQDs-A/S at concentrations of 10 and 100 µg/mL for 24 h. Following the treatment, cells were lysed using RIPA buffer at 4 °C to obtain total protein extracts, and protein concentrations were determined using a BCA Protein Assay Kit (Visual Protein, TPE, Taiwan). The extracted proteins were then separated using SDS-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (10 % gels), and subsequently transferred onto membranes for further analysis.

Blots were incubated with primary antibodies against heme oxygenase-1 (HO-1) (Cell Signaling Technology, MA, USA), NF-κB (Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA), zonula occludens-2 (ZO-2; Santa Cruz, Inc., CA, USA), α1-antitrypsin (AAT; Invitrogen, MA, USA), and β-actin (Sigma-Aldrich, MO, USA) dilutions ranging from 1:500 to 1:5000. Blots were subsequently incubated with HRP-linked secondary antibodies specific to either rabbit or mouse (diluted 1:5000). Protein signals were then detected using an enhanced chemiluminescence (ECL) system (GE Healthcare, IL, USA), with β-actin as the loading control.

Male BALB/c mice (8–12 weeks old) were procured from the National Laboratory Animal Center (TPE, Taiwan) and allowed to acclimate for two weeks in the animal facility of National Formosa University (YUN, Taiwan). All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the guidelines approved by the Animal Control Committee of National Formosa University (A109–1). The mice were maintained under standardized conditions, including a reversed 12-h light/dark cycle, 41 % ± 2 % relative humidity, and a stable temperature of 23 °C ± 1 °C. Food and water were supplied ad libitum. For experimentation, six mice (n = 6) were randomly allocated into different treatment groups, each receiving a single oral gavage dose of CQDs-A, CQDs-S, or CQDs-A/S at 2 g/kg. After 24 h, the mice were euthanized, and their colon and duodenal tissues were harvested for further examination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Serum analysis of calcium (CA), phosphorus (P), and magnesium (MG) in mice
ce:section.ce:para: Blood samples were obtained from CQD-exposed mice, and the serum was separated and preserved at −80 °C for subsequent analysis. The concentrations of CA, P, and MG in the sera from different experimental groups were measured using a Vitros autoanalyzer (Johnson & Johnson, New Brunswick, USA) using commercially available detection kits.

Colon and duodenal tissue samples were preserved in 10 % buffered formalin before the dehydration process and embedding in paraffin wax. Tissue sections of 3 mm were prepared using a rotary microtome. Hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining was performed following a standard protocol to assess the tissue morphology.

All experiments were performed in triplicates. Statistical evaluation was conducted using one-way ANOVA followed by Dunnett’s post hoc test for multiple comparisons. A p-value <0.05 was regarded as statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxic effects on Caco-2 cells after exposure to CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs used in this study exhibit distinct physicochemical properties based on their surface charges and particle sizes. The measured zeta potentials of CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S were −24 mV, 21.9 mV, and 2.74 mV (Table S1) (Jiang et al., 2024), respectively, indicating their varying surface charge characteristics. CQDs-A measured 7.6 ± 2.3 nm, whereas CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S measured 4.6 ± 1.1 nm and 4.1 ± 0.6 nm (Fig. S1) (Jiang et al., 2024), respectively. These findings reveal the differences in the structural and surface properties of different CQDs. The physicochemical characteristics of these CQDs, including their particle size distribution, surface charge, and structural properties, have been thoroughly analyzed in our previous studies (Jiang et al., 2024). CQDs-A, CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S exhibited maximum emission at 430, 445 and 450 nm when excited at 365 nm. These results suggest that the emission properties of CQDs can be finely tuned through precursor selection and molecular composition (Table S1). The quantum yields of CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S were 16.1 %, 2.6 %, and 6.2 %, respectively (compared to quinine with a quantum yield of 53 % in 0.1 M H2SO4) (Table S1). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy was employed to qualitatively analyze the chemical structures of the three types of CQDs. For CQDs-A, characteristic absorption bands were observed at 1160 cm⁻¹ and 1600 cm⁻¹, corresponding to C–N and N–H functional groups, respectively, indicative of amine stretching and bending vibrations (Fig. S2) (Chen et al., 2024). CQDs-S exhibited a distinct peak at 850 cm⁻¹, attributed to C–Cl stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of halogen-containing compounds (Fig. S2). Additionally, a prominent absorption band at 1633 cm⁻¹ in CQDs-S was assigned to the bending vibration of the N–H₂ group in amines (Fig. S2). The FTIR spectrum of the hybrid CQDs-A/S displayed the combined characteristic peaks of both CQDs-A and CQDs-S, confirming their compositional integration. All three CQD variants showed broad absorption in the 3200–3640 cm⁻¹ region, corresponding to O–H stretching vibrations of phenolic groups, reflecting the presence of nitrogen- and oxygen-containing surface functionalities (Fig. S2) (Chen et al., 2024). These analyses confirmed the successful synthesis and stability of CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S, providing a strong foundation for their biological evaluation in this study.

The cytotoxicity of CQDs with different surface charges was evaluated in Caco-2 cells using the CCK-8 assay after 24 h of exposure. The tested CQDs included CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S at concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 1000 μg/mL (Fig. 1A). At lower concentrations (0.1–10 μg/mL), all three CQDs exhibited minimal cytotoxicity, with cell viability exceeding 90 % relative to the control, indicating that these doses did not significantly affect cell viability (Fig. 1A). However, at 100 μg/mL, CQDs-S exhibited the lowest cell viability among the three CQDs, suggesting that it had the highest toxicity at this concentration (Fig. 1A). This result indicated that positively charged CQDs-S may have stronger electrostatic interactions with negatively charged cell membranes, leading to enhanced cellular uptake and increased cytotoxicity (Havrdova et al., 2022). At the highest concentration (1000 μg/mL), cytotoxicity was significantly enhanced across all CQDs, with CQDs-S inducing the most severe toxicity, as indicated by the corresponding lowest cell viability (∼30 %) (Fig. 1A). In contrast, 1000 μg/mL CQDs-A/S and CQDs-A exhibited relatively lower toxicity, with cell viabilities of around 80 % and 90 %, respectively (Fig. 1A).

The fluorescence images (Fig. 1B) illustrate the differences in mitochondrial integrity among the treated groups. In functional mitochondria, the JC-1 dye forms aggregates that emit red fluorescence, whereas in depolarized mitochondria, it remains in its monomeric form, producing green fluorescence. In the control and CQDs-A-treated groups (Fig. 1B(a) and (b)), both red and green fluorescence signals were observed at comparable levels, resulting in a yellow-orange color, indicating that CQDs-A did not significantly disrupt the mitochondrial membrane potential. This suggests that negatively charged CQDs-A exert minimal apoptotic effects on Caco-2 cells. Conversely, the CQDs-S (Fig. 1B(c)) and CQDs-A/S (Fig. 1B(d)) treatments led to a pronounced shift toward green fluorescence, with only weak red fluorescence signals detected, indicating depolarized mitochondria. In the merged images, the predominant green color confirmed that CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S significantly disrupted mitochondrial function, inducing apoptosis in Caco-2 cells. The loss of mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm) observed in these two groups indicates higher levels of mitochondrial damage and apoptotic initiation compared with the cells treated with CQDs-A.

The differential cytotoxic effects observed in this study suggest that the surface charge plays a critical role in determining the toxicity of the CQDs. CQDs-S, with a positive charge (+21.9 mV), exhibited the highest cytotoxicity, likely due to their strong electrostatic interactions with cell membranes, facilitating higher cellular uptake and the induction of oxidative stress or membrane damage (Havrdova et al., 2022; Havrdová et al., 2021). In contrast, CQDs-A (−24 mV) and CQDs-A/S (2.74 mV) demonstrated comparatively lower cytotoxicity, possibly due to differences in aggregation behavior or cellular uptake mechanisms (Fröhlich, 2012). Overall, these findings highlight that CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S induce mitochondrial dysfunction and apoptosis in Caco-2 cells, whereas CQDs-A maintains the mitochondrial integrity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidative and inflammatory responses after exposure to CQDs in Caco-2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the oxidative stress and inflammatory responses induced by CQDs with different surface charges, intracellular ROS accumulation, HO-1 protein expression, and NF-κB protein expression were measured in Caco-2 cells after 24 h of exposure to the CQDs. The ROS levels were assessed using the DCFH-DA assay at CQD concentrations of 10, 100, and 1000 μg/mL, whereas HO-1 and NF-κB protein expression were analyzed using western blotting at concentrations of 10 and 100 μg/mL. At 10 μg/mL, all three CQD treatments (CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S) resulted in minimal ROS accumulation, with levels comparable to those of the controls (Fig. 2A). However, at 100 μg/mL, a significant increase in ROS production was observed for all CQDs (p < 0.05), with CQDs-S (green bars in Fig. 2A) inducing the highest ROS accumulation, followed by CQDs-A and then CQDs-A/S (Fig. 2A). At the highest concentration (1000 μg/mL), CQDs-S caused an acute increase in the ROS level (∼40-fold compared to those of the controls); this was significantly higher than the increase induced by CQDs-A and CQDs-A/S, suggesting a dose-dependent oxidative stress response (Fig. 2A) for CQDs-S. This result indicated that CQDs-S, which has a strong positive surface charge, may enhance cellular uptake and result in excessive ROS generation, causing oxidative damage (Zhang and Monteiro-Riviere, 2009).

The levels of HO-1, an oxidative stress-responsive protein, were assessed to determine the cellular defense mechanisms against CQD-induced oxidative stress (Consoli et al., 2021). Compared with the controls, CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S all significantly upregulated HO-1 expression at concentrations of 10 and 100 μg/mL, confirming their ability to induce oxidative stress-related cellular responses (Fig. 2B). Among the tested CQDs, CQDs-S at 100 μg/mL induced the highest HO-1 expression (∼3.5-fold increase), followed by CQDs-A/S and CQDs-A (Fig. 2B). This trend aligned with the ROS accumulation data, supporting the hypothesis that of the tested CQDs, CQDs-S generate the most oxidative stress and activate HO-1 as an adaptive antioxidant response.

To further examine the inflammatory response, NF-κB expression was evaluated. Compared with that of the controls, all CQDs treatment resulted in a significant increase in NF-κB expression at concentrations of 10 and 100 μg/mL, confirming the activation of inflammation-related pathways (Fig. 3 ). CQDs-S at 10 and 100 μg/mL induced the highest NF-κB expression, followed by CQDs-A/S and CQDs-A (Fig. 3). This suggests that CQDs-S induce stronger proinflammatory responses, potentially due to their higher oxidative stress generation, which could activate NF-κB signaling (Bellezza et al., 2012; Choi and Alam, 1996). The differences observed in ROS generation, HO-1 upregulation, and NF-κB activation indicate that the surface charge plays a crucial role in CQD-induced cellular stress responses. CQDs-S (+21.9 mV) exhibited the highest oxidative stress and inflammatory response, likely due to enhanced electrostatic interactions with negatively charged cellular components, which subsequently promoted greater ROS production and NF-κB activation (Manke et al., 2013; Sun et al., 2019). In contrast, CQDs-A (−24 mV) induced relatively lower ROS and NF-κB expression, possibly due to reduced cellular uptake or weaker interactions with intracellular organelles (Manke et al., 2013). CQDs-A/S (2.74 mV) induced an intermediate response, suggesting that the near-neutral surface charge modulates oxidative stress and inflammation to a moderate extent. Overall, these findings highlight that CQDs-S induced the strongest oxidative stress and inflammatory responses, whereas CQDs-A exhibited the weakest cytotoxic effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intestinal dysfunction after exposure to CQDs in Caco-2 cells
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the potential impact of CQDs on intestinal barrier function and IBD risk, ZO-2 and AAT protein expression was analyzed using western blotting, whereas TEER measurements were conducted to assess barrier integrity. The CQD concentrations used for ZO-2 and AAT protein analysis were 10 and 100 μg/mL, with an exposure time of 24 h, while TEER measurements were performed at 10 μg/mL over a 3-day period. ZO-2 (zonula occludens-2) is a key tight junction protein involved in maintaining epithelial barrier integrity (Alizadeh et al., 2022; Raya-Sandino et al., 2017). Compared with that of the control, the CQDs treatments did not significantly alter ZO-2 protein expression, suggesting that none of the tested CQDs induced major disruptions in tight junction integrity at the tested concentrations (Fig. 4A). However, a slight reduction in ZO-2 expression was observed for CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S at 10 and 100 μg/mL, indicating that higher concentrations, these CQDs may have a mild impact on tight junction stability (Fig. 4A). TEER values are widely used quantitative indicators of intestinal epithelial barrier integrity. As shown in Fig. 4B, the TEER values remained relatively stable over the 3-day period for all CQD-treated groups compared with those of the control, with CQDs-A showing the least fluctuation and CQDs-S exhibiting more variability (Fig. 4B). No significant decrease in TEER values was observed, suggesting that CQD exposure at 10 μg/mL does not severely compromise the integrity of the Caco-2 cell barrier over time (Fig. 4B). The minor fluctuations in the CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S groups may indicate slight transient effects on the barrier permeability.

AAT is an anti-inflammatory protein involved in regulating intestinal immune responses and protecting against inflammatory conditions such as IBD (Yang et al., 2000b). As shown in Fig. 5 , at 100 μg/mL, the CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S treatments resulted in a noticeable reduction in AAT protein expression, whereas CQDs-A showed the least impact on AAT levels. This suggests that CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S may suppress anti-inflammatory defense mechanisms, potentially increasing the susceptibility to inflammation-related intestinal disorders (Ballesio et al., 2024). The reduction in AAT expression aligns with the observed slight decrease in ZO-2 levels, further suggesting that CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S may have a more pronounced impact on epithelial homeostasis compared with CQDs-A.

The combined findings from the ZO-2 expression, TEER measurement, and AAT expression analyses suggest that CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S exhibit a greater tendency to affect intestinal barrier function and inflammatory responses compared with that of CQDs-A. The positive surface charge of CQDs-S may contribute to stronger interactions with cell membranes, potentially resulting in minor disruptions in tight junction stability and reduced AAT expression, which could increase the risk of intestinal inflammation (Fröhlich, 2012; Yang et al., 2000a). In contrast, CQDs-A exhibited minimal effects on epithelial integrity, possibly due to lower cellular uptake and weaker interactions with tight junction proteins (Fröhlich, 2012; Yang et al., 2000a). Although no significant barrier dysfunction was observed at the tested concentrations, the reduction in AAT expression caused by CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S suggests the potential risk of exacerbating inflammatory conditions upon long-term exposure (Yang et al., 2000a). These findings highlight the importance of surface charge modulation when designing CQDs for biomedical applications, particularly in gastrointestinal-related fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs induced injury in the intestine of BALB/c mice
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the intestinal toxicity of CQDs (2 g/kg), an acute toxicity test was conducted in BALB/c mice. Blood samples of treated mice were collected 24 h post-administration for the analysis of calcium (Ca), phosphorus (P), and magnesium (Mg) levels. These minerals play crucial roles in maintaining intestinal homeostasis, barrier integrity, and overall gut function. Calcium, a critical component for bone health and cellular signaling, exhibited a notable decrease in the CQDs-S- treated group compared with that in the control (Fig. 6A). This reduction in calcium levels might suggest an interference by CQDs-S with calcium metabolism or absorption, which could potentially result in negative effects on intestinal integrity and function. Decreased calcium levels can adversely affect the epithelial barrier function and increase the risk of inflammation within the intestinal tract (Rios-Arce et al., 2017).

Conversely, the phosphorus levels were significantly higher in the CQDs-S group than in the control (Fig. 6B). An elevation in the level of phosphorus, which is vital for energy production and cellular structure, indicates a possible alteration in phosphate metabolism that could enhance ATP synthesis (Kritmetapak and Kumar 2021). While this might seem beneficial, excessively high phosphorus levels can disrupt the delicate balance of minerals in the body and potentially result in complications such as calcification of non-skeletal tissues (Kritmetapak and Kumar 2021; Vorland et al., 2017). The magnesium levels remained stable across all groups, including the control and those treated with CQDs (Fig. 6C). As magnesium is essential for numerous biological processes, including those related to DNA replication and repair, enzyme function, and neuromuscular excitability (Fiorentini et al., 2021), its stability suggests that under the conditions of this acute toxicity study, CQDs did not significantly impact the homeostasis of this mineral.

The distinct effects of CQDs-S on the calcium and phosphorus levels highlight the complex interactions that nanoparticles can have with mineral homeostasis. The reduction in calcium levels paired with an increase in those of phosphorus raises concerns about potential long-term effects of CQDs on bone health and cardiovascular risk, given the known risks associated with altered levels of these minerals (Kritmetapak and Kumar 2021; Rios-Arce et al., 2017). The stability of the magnesium levels suggests that acute exposure to CQDs does not disrupt all aspects of mineral homeostasis; however, the specific interactions with the calcium and phosphorus pathways warrant further investigation (Fiorentini et al., 2021). Overall, the findings suggest that while CQDs-A and CQDs-A/S do not significantly alter mineral levels, CQDs-S may pose more substantial risks due to its impact on calcium and phosphorus metabolism. This study underscores the importance of considering the broader systemic effects of nanoparticles beyond their local or targeted site of action, particularly in the context of their use in medical applications where long-term exposure and systemic distribution are likely.

To evaluate the possible histopathological impact of CQDs (2 g/kg) on intestinal tissues, colon and duodenum samples from BALB/c mice were subjected to H&E staining after acute toxicity tests. In the control group (Fig. 7A and E), the colon and duodenum exhibited normal mucosal structures, with intact epithelial linings, well-organized crypt structures, and no inflammation or tissue damage. Similarly, in the CQDs-A (Fig. 7B and F), CQDs-S (Fig. 7C and G), and CQDs-A/S (Fig. 7D and H) treated groups, the intestinal architecture remained unaltered, with no evidence of epithelial erosion, crypt distortion, inflammatory infiltration, or villous atrophy.

These results indicate that none of the tested CQDs induced histopathological alterations in the colon or duodenum, even at a high dose of 2 g/kg. Despite previous findings suggesting that CQDs-S and CQDs-A/S may cause oxidative stress and minor disruptions in intestinal homeostasis, no observable tissue damage or inflammation was detected in the histological analysis. This suggests that CQD exposure at the tested dose does not cause direct morphological damage to the intestinal epithelium in acute toxicity settings. Although CQDs did not induce structural changes in the intestinal tissues, further investigations are necessary to evaluate the long-term exposure effects and potential functional impairments, particularly regarding intestinal permeability, microbiome alterations, and chronic inflammatory responses.

In this study, we evaluated the cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, inflammation, intestinal barrier integrity, and in vivo acute toxicity of CQDs with different surface charges (CQDs-A, CQDs-S, and CQDs-A/S) in Caco-2 cells and BALB/c mice. Surface charge plays a crucial role in CQD-induced biological effects. CQDs-S, with a surface charge of +21.9 mV, showed the highest cytotoxicity, oxidative stress, and inflammatory activation, significantly reducing cell viability, mitochondrial function, and intestinal barrier proteins. Meanwhile, CQDs-A, with a surface charge of −24 mV, exhibited the least toxicity, whereas CQDs-A/S (surface charge: 2.74 mV) exerted intermediate effects. In vivo, CQDs-S disrupted Ca and P homeostasis, although histopathological analysis revealed no structural damage at the tested dose (2 g/kg). These findings highlight the necessity for surface charge modulation to minimize toxicity and inflammation risks for biomedical applications. Future studies should examine the long-term effects of CQDs use on intestinal permeability, gut microbiome composition, and chronic inflammation.

Yi-Chun Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Yan-Yu Hong: Resources, Investigation. Hsin Her Yu: Visualization, Methodology. Kuei-Chun Liu: Resources, Investigation. Da-Li Wang: Resources, Investigation. Wen-Kai Kuo: Visualization, Methodology. Ya-Wen Wang: Investigation. Chia-Hua Lin: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC, Taiwan) [grant number NSTC 110-2811-E-150-005 and 111-2622-E-150-003].

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.hazadv.2025.100774.
